transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
Sore throat.
When did your sore throat start?
2 days ago.
Any fever, chills, or difficulty swallowing?
Fever and chills. No difficulties swallowing.
Any cough, nasal congestion, or runny nose?
No.
Any swollen glands or rash?
Swollen glands, yes, but no rash.
Any exposure to sick contacts or recent travel?
Yes.
Any difficulty breathing or chest pain?
No.
Have you noticed any white patches or pus on your tonsils or throat?
White patches.
Have you taken any medications or treatments for your sore throat so far?
The only thing I did was some tea with honey, and that really hasn't done much.
Any history of rheumatic fever or heart valve problems?
No.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

It sounds like you have symptoms consistent with a bacterial throat infection, likely strep throat. Because this is a phone visit, I can’t examine you or do tests, but based on what you told me, medication treatment may be appropriate. A licensed physician will review this note and, if appropriate, may prescribe antibiotics that are safe for you given your penicillin allergy. In the meantime, you can use acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain and fever if you can take them safely. If you develop trouble breathing, severe throat pain, difficulty swallowing, or a very high fever, please seek urgent in-person care the same day. If you notice signs of airway blockage or severe illness, go to the emergency room right away. If your symptoms persist or worsen, getting tested in person for strep throat can help guide treatment. A physician will review your case and follow up as needed.
